subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: This guide helps you teach a young child (age 5 to 6) all about sounds. Your child cannot read much yet, so you will do the talking, the singing, and the listening games together. You read the words aloud, and the child looks, listens, points, and plays. Warmth and fun matter more than getting every answer right.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Science 0097 — Stage 1 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Say that sounds are made when things shake or vibrate.
Name the ear as the body part we use to hear.
Sort sounds into loud and quiet.
Describe the sounds we hear around us.
Listen carefully and identify different sounds.

concepts.title: What Is Sound?
concepts.explanation: This guide helps you teach a young child (age 5 to 6) all about sounds. Your child cannot read much yet, so you will do the talking, the singing, and the listening games together. You read the words aloud, and the child looks, listens, points, and plays. Warmth and fun matter more than getting every answer right.
concepts.example: Sounds are everywhere. When your child wakes up, they may hear the adhan, the call to prayer, floating across the morning air. They hear a rooster crowing, a goat bleating, birds singing, and the soft splash of water…

concepts.title: How We Hear
concepts.explanation: Sounds are everywhere. When your child wakes up, they may hear the adhan, the call to prayer, floating across the morning air. They hear a rooster crowing, a goat bleating, birds singing, and the soft splash of water from the well. In this unit your child will learn where sounds come from, how we make sounds, how our ears help us hear, and how to keep our ears safe from very loud sounds. Take one small part each day. There is no hurry.
concepts.example: This unit is spread across five gentle lessons. You do not have to do a whole lesson in one sitting. Young children learn best in short bursts of ten or fifteen minutes, with lots of play in between. Do a little, then…

concepts.title: Loud Sounds
concepts.explanation: This unit is spread across five gentle lessons. You do not have to do a whole lesson in one sitting. Young children learn best in short bursts of ten or fifteen minutes, with lots of play in between. Do a little, then come back to it. Repeat the same game many times. Children love repetition, and each time they understand a little more.
concepts.example: Every lesson has the same simple shape. First there is a short list of steps for you to follow. Then there is a 'Say to the Child' part, with simple words you can read straight out loud. Then there are questions to ask…

concepts.title: Quiet Sounds
concepts.explanation: Every lesson has the same simple shape. First there is a short list of steps for you to follow. Then there is a 'Say to the Child' part, with simple words you can read straight out loud. Then there are questions to ask and a fun fact or tip. You are the teacher, the guide, and the playmate all at once. Keep your voice warm and your face happy, and your child will love learning about sounds with you.
concepts.example: Do not worry if your child gives a 'wrong' answer. At this age, guessing, wondering, and trying are exactly what we want. Praise the effort. Say things like, 'Good listening!' and 'What a clever guess!' The goal is…

concepts.title: Making Sounds
concepts.explanation: Do not worry if your child gives a 'wrong' answer. At this age, guessing, wondering, and trying are exactly what we want. Praise the effort. Say things like, 'Good listening!' and 'What a clever guess!' The goal is curiosity, not correctness.
concepts.example: Say each word aloud and let your child repeat it. Do not worry about spelling. These are just for talking and listening.

concepts.title: Sounds Around Us
concepts.explanation: Say each word aloud and let your child repeat it. Do not worry about spelling. These are just for talking and listening.

activities.title: Listen for One Minute
steps: Follow the investigation plan in your experiments book.

activities.title: Shake a Sound Maker
steps: Follow the investigation plan in your experiments book.

activities.title: Loud and Quiet Sorting
steps: Follow the investigation plan in your experiments book.

activities.title: Feel a Drum Vibrate
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of quiet sounds around your home or outside.
Watch it closely for a few minutes and note three things you notice.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

activities.title: Make a String Buzz
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of making sounds around your home or outside.
Find examples at home and sort them into groups, then explain your rule.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

activities.title: Guess That Sound
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of sounds around us around your home or outside.
Change one thing, keep everything else the same, and record what happens.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

terms: What we hear when something shakes the air
A fast shaking that makes sound
The body part we hear with
How loud or quiet a sound is
To pay attention with your ears
A sound that bounces back to you

vocabulary.meaning: What we hear when something shakes the air
A fast shaking that makes sound
The body part we hear with
How loud or quiet a sound is
To pay attention with your ears
A sound that bounces back to you

commonMistakes: Every sound is made by something shaking or vibrating — a drum skin, a string, or your voice.
Loud sounds always come from near by.
Far-away thunder can still be loud; sounds get quieter as they travel further.

connections.text: You hear with your ears — one of the five senses you learned about.
Drums, strings and your own voice all make sound by shaking.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Quiet Sounds.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Every lesson has the same simple shape. First there is a short list of steps for you to follow. Then there is a 'Say to the Child' part, with simple words you can read straight out loud. Then there are questions to ask and a fun fact or tip. You are the teacher, the guide, and the playmate all at once. Keep your voice warm and your face happy, and your child will love learning about sounds with you.

questions.question: We hear with our...
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: We use our ears to hear sounds.

questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Banging a drum hard makes a loud sound.

questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: A whisper is a very quiet sound.

questions.question: Sounds are made when things...
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Sounds are made when things vibrate — they shake very fast.

questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: A shouting crowd makes a loud sound.

questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Listening quietly helps us hear soft sounds.

questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Loud means A big, strong sound.

rounds.explanation: Sounds are made when things vibrate — they shake very fast.

rounds.prompt: Which science word matches this meaning: To pay attention with your ears?
rounds.clue: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
rounds.explanation: Listen means To pay attention with your ears.

games.title: Mastery Mix: Ear
games.description: Practise ear through four short challenges.

rounds.prompt: Which science word matches this meaning: The body part we hear with?
rounds.clue: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
rounds.explanation: Ear means The body part we hear with.

rounds.prompt: A drum banged hard makes a...
rounds.clue: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
rounds.explanation: Banging a drum hard makes a loud sound.

rounds.prompt: Which is a loud sound?
rounds.clue: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
rounds.explanation: A shouting crowd makes a loud sound.

rounds.prompt: Which science word matches this meaning: A sound that bounces back to you?
rounds.clue: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
rounds.explanation: Echo means A sound that bounces back to you.

games.title: Unit Champion: Loud
games.description: Practise loud through four short challenges.

rounds.prompt: Which science word matches this meaning: A big, strong sound?
rounds.clue: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
rounds.explanation: Loud means A big, strong sound.

rounds.prompt: A whisper is a...
rounds.clue: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
rounds.explanation: A whisper is a very quiet sound.

rounds.prompt: To hear a soft sound better, we should...
rounds.clue: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
rounds.explanation: Listening quietly helps us hear soft sounds.

rounds.prompt: Which science word matches this meaning: What we hear when something shakes the air?
rounds.clue: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
rounds.explanation: Sound means What we hear when something shakes the air.

selfAssessment: I can say that sounds are made when things shake or vibrate.
I can name the ear as the body part we use to hear.
I can sort sounds into loud and quiet.
I can make sounds in different ways.
I can describe the sounds we hear around us.
I can listen carefully and identify different sounds.
